title: - WHAT TO PRUNE AND WHAT NOT TO PRUNE AT INI TIALIZATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - WHAT TO PRUNE AND WHAT NOT TO PRUNE AT INI TIALIZATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Post-training dropout based approaches achieve high sparsity and are well established means of deciphering problems relating to computational cost and overfitting in Neural Network architectures citesrivastava2014dropout, (Pan et al., 2016), Zhu & Gupta (2017), LeCun et al. (1990). Contrastingly, pruning at initialization is still far behind Frankle et al. (2020). Initialization pruning is more efficacious when it comes to scaling computation cost of the network. Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout. It is also averse to retraining losses.

In approbation of the above reasons, the paper presents two approaches to prune at initialization. The goal is to achieve higher sparsity while preserving performance. 1) K-starts, begins with k random p-sparse matrices at initialization. In the first couple of epochs the network then determines the ”fittest” of these p-sparse matrices in an attempt to find the ”lottery ticket” Frankle & Carbin (2018) p-sparse network. The approach is adopted from how evolutionary algorithms find the best individual. Depending on the Neural Network architecture, fitness criteria can be based on magnitude of network weights, magnitude of gradient accumulation over an epoch or a combination of both. 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs. Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance of the network. Contrarily, the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity. 3) Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts outperforms either methods and random dropout consistently.

The benefits of using the provided pertaining approaches are: 1) They do not require specific knowledge of the classification task, fixing of dropout threshold or regularization parameters 2) Retraining of the model is neither necessary nor affects the performance of the p-sparse network.
We evaluate the efficacy of the said methods on Autoencoders and Fully Connected Multilayered Perceptrons. The datasets used are MNIST and Fashion MNIST.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Computational complexity and overfitting in neural networks is a well established problem Frankle & Carbin (2018), Han et al. (2015), LeCun et al. (1990), Denil et al. (2013). We utilize pruning approaches for the following two reasons: 1) To reduce the computational cost of a fully connected neural network. 2) To reduce overfitting in the network.

Given a large number of post-training pruning approaches Srivastava et al. (2014), Geman et al. (1992), Pan et al. (2016), the paper attempts to propose two pre-training pruning approaches: kstarts and dissipating gradients. Moreover, it appears to be the case that when isolated from other factors sparse networks outperform fully connected networks. When not isolated they perform at least as well up to a percentage of sparsity depending on the number of parameters in the said network. kstarts and dissipating gradients provide are simple nevertheless effective methods to quickly look for best sparse networks to.
The approaches exploit the knowledge that a network has multiple underlying p-sparse networks that perform just as well and in some cases even better when contrasted with their fully connected

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

counterparts Frankle & Carbin (2018). What percentage of sparsity is realized, depends largely on the number of parameters originally present in the network. Such sparse networks are potent in preventing over-fitting and reducing computational cost.

The poset-training pruning has several approaches in place such as adding various regularization schemes to prune the network Louizos et al. (2017), Pan et al. (2016) or using second derivative or hessian of the weights for dropout LeCun et al. (1990), Hassibi & Stork (1993). Han et al. (2015), Alford et al. (2019), Zhu & Gupta (2017) use an efficient iterative pruning method to iteratively increase sparsity. Srivastava et al. (2014) dropout random hidden units with p probability instead of weights to avoid overfitting in general. Each of these approaches is effective and achieves good sparsity post-training.

We use a simple intuitive models that achieve good results and exploits the fact that a number of sub networks in a Neural Network has the potential to individually learn the input Srivastava et al. (2014). We decide on a sparse network early on based on the dropout method and use only that for training. This provides an edge for faster computation, quicker elimination of excess weights and reduced generalization error. The sparsity achieved is superior to random dropout.

Section II gives a general introduction to all the methods, section III defines p-sparsity, section IV provides the algorithm for both approaches, section V describes experimental setup and results, section VI discusses various design choices, section VII gives a general discussion of results, section VIII discusses limitations of the approach and section IX provides conclusions and final remarks.

## 2 PRUNING METHODS

## 2.1 KSTARTS

## 2.1.1 KSTARTS AND EVOLUTIONARY ALGORITHMS

We take the concept of k random starts from Evolutionary Algorithms (Vikhar, 2016) that use a fitness function or heuristic to perform ”natural selection” in optimization and search based problems (Goldberg & Holland, 1988). It is relatively simple to fit genetic algorithms to the problem at hand. Other method that would be equally effective with a little bit of modification are Hunting Search (Oftadeh et al., 2010), Natural Adaptation Strategies (Wierstra et al., 2008), firefly algorithm (Yang, 2010) etc.

The basic components of the algorithm are: (1) **Population** : A product of network weights and sparse matrices. (2) **Individual** : An instance of the population. (3) **Fitness Function** : The heuristic chosen for evaluation of the population.

## 2.1.2 POPULATION

We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation **??** . In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration. Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.

_population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_

(1)

## 2.1.3 INDIVIDUAL

Each individual, in a population of K instances, is a sparse matrix of size equal to the size of network weights, W. The number of 0’s and 1’s in the sparsity matrix are determined by the connectivity factor p which is further described in section 3. An sparse matrix of _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5 will have _≈_ 50% 0’s and _≈_ 50% 1s.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 2.1.4 EVALUATION/FITNESS FUNCTION

The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.

## 2.1.5 NEXT GENERATION SELECTION
Assuming each iteration is the next generation. In each generation the fit individual is favoured so:

- The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.

- Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.

## 2.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS

Magnitude and gradient based pruning approaches are popular in post-training pruning Srivastava et al. (2014), LeCun et al. (1990). It does not make much sense to employ them pre-training becauseof the randomly generated weights. But in order to reduce error, any network aims to target updating weights that influence the results most. Based upon that hypothesis, in each epoch we sum gradients over and eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated. In equation 3 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch. In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.

One consideration in this approach is to not do this for too many epochs which can be only 2 if the image is very monochrome and more than 2 if the gradients are dissipating more slowly. Moreover, once specific weights have reached their optimal learning, their gradients will dissipate and we don’t want to eliminate them.

## 2.3 COMBINATION DROPOUT

Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well. The approach achieves better performance than either methods.

## 3 DEFINING P-SPARSITY

In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated. Figure 1 shows a fully connected conventional neural network (figure 1a) and three sparsely connected networks with different values of p (figures 1b, 1c, 1d).

## 3.1 CONNECTIVITY FACTOR

Connectivity factor, p, determines the percentage of connections to be removed between any two layers of the network. For instance if p = 0.0 than the network is be fully connected as shown in figure 1a, if on the opposite extreme, p=1.0, then there will be no connections between two layers of neurons. If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain. If p=0.3, figure 1c, approximately 70% of the connection still exist and if p=0.7, figure 1d, a mere 30% of the connections are active between two network layers. In short p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix shown in equation **??** .

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

(a) A fully connected (b) A sample 0.5- (c) A sample 0.3- (d) A sample 0.7-
or 0.0-Sparse network Sparse network layer Sparse network layer Sparse network layer
layer
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 1: Sample P-Sparse Network Layers

## 4 ALGORITHM

The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.

## 4.1 KSTARTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 1 gives the kstarts method:

1. We have K number of sparse matrices as shown in equation **??** generated and we call them KI in algorithm 1.

2. Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.

3. Every 5 iterations, the individual with lowest fitness is dropped.

**Algorithm 1:** k Random starts

**input :** Data, params,K,p **output:** W,b initialize W,b; _KI ←_ K individuals with approximately 1-p percent active connections; **for** _maxiterations_ **do** Run Neural Network; Update weights; **if** _One individual in KI is left_ **then** W _←_ individual; **else** W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights); **for** _every 5 iterations_ **do** pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; **end end end**

- 4.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 2 is more simple and just eliminates weights with sum of gradients equal to zero in the first 1-4 epochs depending on the desired sparsity.

Figure 2: Performance of dropout methods with increasing sparsity.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS AND RESULTS

The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST Deng (2012) and Fashion MNIST Xiao et al. (2017). The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.

The Architecture used for learning curves is a single layered NN(784-10).

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) Dataset : Fashion MNIST (b) Dataset : MNIST
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 3: Effect of sample size on learning when varying the sparsity of the network with kstarts. Each trial involves running a p-sparse network on a fixed training set of N examples from dataset for 2.5k iterations and is averaged over 10 trials each.

## 5.1 EFFECT OF INCREASING SPARSITY

As sparsity increases, overall performance reduces. Figure 2 shows the behaviour of various dropout methods presented in this paper.

In case of random dropout, it’s indeed a random shot. Either no useful weight is eliminated or multiple crucial weights are eliminated which decides how well does random dropout perform. Kstarts performs slightly better on average with multiple start choices. Depending on how many independent p-sparse networks are present in the network that can learn well, one of them can be identified, given k is large enough and the fitness function is smartly decided by first examining the weights and gradients.

Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn’t learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs. It’s also most reliable. Combination works by far the best because it does not only rely on eliminating weights that are not being updated but also uses kstarts. It seems to achieve superior performance as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on.

## 5.2 VARIATION IN SAMPLE SIZE

Figures 3a and 3b show relationship of varying sample size to different sparsity values in a single layer NN over 2.5k iterations.

The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters, hidden units and various design choices, kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network. The standard deviation is also lower to fully connected network as well as random dropout. kstarts dropout also learns faster than a fully connected network. For instance, if the iterations for the experiments in figure 3a and 3b are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.

## 6 DESIGN PARAMETER CHOICES

There are a number of design parameter choices for the algorithm 1 presented here. Some are explained in detail in this section.

## 6.1 CHOICE OF FITNESS FUNCTION

Since the fitness function determines the individual being passed on to the next generation and the individual being eliminated. We had three choices for choosing the fitness of an individual each with it’s own pros and cons.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
- **Gradient:** The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

- _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)

- doing so can have fitness totally dependant on the current update and the new weights are heavily skewed towards the performance of the current iterations which again doesn’t seem that appropriate.
- **Sum of Gradients:** The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.

## 6.2 NO. OF LAYERS AND NO. HIDDEN UNITS

We initially use single layer NN (784-10) to isolate the effects of kstarts algorithm from effects of other parameters that may have a large impact on performance as the size of the network grows. Those parameters i.e. number of hidden layers, regularization choices, types of layers may aid or adversely effect the performance of the algorithm and by performing the experiments on the basic unit of a neural network we were able to concur that the method is effective in it’s own right. After concluding the approach works we tested on three layered NN and Autoencoder.

## 6.3 COST EFFECTIVESNESS

One beneficial feature of pre-training pruning approaches is that the best p-sparse network is quickly identified. There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluc¸ & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks. Although that is out of scope for our findings.

## 7 DISCUSSION

- 7.1 EFFECT OF K

1. From the experiments done so far lower K ( _≈_ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( _≈_ 100). This can be because the more times a different matrix is chosen by the network, the more times the network has to adopt to learning with only that p-sparse network.

## 7.2 EFFECT OF P

1. A sparse network can outperforms a fully connected network for lower number of iterations and smaller networks.

2. An appropriate value of p, for instance in these experiments _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5, seems to work best for random dropout, kstarts dropout and combination dropout. A poor choice of p can’t seem to be remedied by a better choice of K.

3. p can be thought of as an information limiter. For better learning, if the network is only provided with particular features, it might have an easier time learning the class specific features in different nodes but this only remains a wishful speculation and requires further analysis. Table 1 shows relationship between k,p and no of iterations in a single layer NN (784-10)

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Table 1: MNIST-Mean Accuracy and standard deviation averaged over 10 runs

## 8 LIMITATIONS

There are a number of limitations to the approach and further investigation is required in a number of domains.
1. The NNs used are single and three layered feed forward networks and autoencoders. CNNs are not experimented upon.
2. Only classification tasks are considered and those on only two datasets: MNIST and Fashion MNIST.
3. Ideally using sparse matrix should make for efficient computation but since the algorithms for that are not used it at this point does not show what the time comparison of the approaches will be.

## 9 CONCLUSIONS AND FINAL REMARKS

We present two methods for pruning weights pre-training or in the first couple of epochs. The comparisons are made against random dropout and both approaches mostly perform better than the random dropout. We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches. A lot more analysis of the approach is required on multiple datasets, learning tasks and network architectures but the basic methods seem to be effective.

## REFERENCES

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper explores pruning neural networks at initialization using evolutionary-inspired selection (K-starts) and gradient monitoring. While the premise is aligned with current interests in the 'Lottery Ticket Hypothesis', the technical execution is hampered by several formal inconsistencies, particularly in the definition of the K-starts fitness function and the scheduling of weight updates. The methodology section contains conflicting information regarding how and when weights are updated, and the fitness function as defined lacks the mathematical grounding to explain why it would effectively identify 'winning tickets' beyond simple magnitude reinforcement. Furthermore, the claim that sparse networks outperform fully connected ones on single layers is presented without sufficient discussion of the regularization effects that typically explain such phenomena in small-data/small-iteration regimes.

comments.title: Mathematical Inconsistency in Fitness Function Definition
comments.quote: fittestIndividual = max(\sum_{i,c} ind_{i,c})
comments.explanation: Equation 2 defines the fittest individual as the one with the maximum sum of its elements. Earlier, Section 2.1.3 defines an individual as a sparse matrix (mask) where elements are 0 or 1. If every individual has the same 'p-sparsity' (as stated in 2.1.3), the sum of 1s in every mask will be approximately the same (p * total parameters). This makes the fitness function constant across the population, rendering the selection process arbitrary. If the sum is instead meant to be over the masked weights (W * K-Sparse), the formula fails to include the weight matrix 'W' defined in Equation 1. I checked if the 'sum of weights' mentioned in Algorithm 1 resolved this, but it creates a circular dependency: we select the mask based on weight magnitude, but the weights only exist where the mask allows them to. This logic likely leads to 'rich-get-richer' selection of masks that happen to overlap with large initial weights, which is not clearly justified as an evolutionary strategy.

comments.title: Contradiction in Weight Update and Selection Timing
comments.quote: Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W
comments.explanation: There is a significant conflict between the text in 4.1 and Algorithm 1. The text suggests that the K-starts selection replaces the standard gradient update ('instead of the usual gradient update'). However, Algorithm 1 shows 'Update weights' (Step 6) occurring *before* the fitness selection (Step 8-11). If the selection happens every iteration as per the text, but the population pruning happens every 5 iterations as per the algorithm, it is unclear how the gradients are applied to the 'K' different individuals. If the network only maintains one set of weights 'W', the other 'K-1' individuals are never trained, meaning their fitness cannot improve or be evaluated meaningfully against the data. This makes the reproduction of the 'evolutionary' aspect of the algorithm impossible.

comments.title: Numerical/Notation Error in Dissipating Gradients
comments.quote: accumulated d W < 0 . 0001 then accumulated d W ← 0 ; else accumulated d W ← 1;
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 and Equation 4 describe a pruning mask creation where weights with small accumulated gradients are set to 0. However, the logic 'accumulated dW < 0.0001' is applied to the sum of gradients. In standard deep learning, gradients can be positive or negative. A weight with large but opposing gradient updates would have an 'accumulated' sum near zero, leading to it being pruned despite being highly active. Standard practice (e.g., in SNIP or magnitude pruning) uses the absolute value or the square of gradients. The authors might have intended absolute sum, but as written, the method would incorrectly prune weights with high variance but zero mean gradients.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of p-sparsity
comments.quote: In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated.
comments.explanation: The paper is inconsistent in its definition of 'p'. In Section 3, it says 'p percent... are eliminated' (meaning p=0.7 is 70% sparse). However, Figure 1 caption says 'a sample 0.7-Sparse network' for a visualization that appears to show a sparse network, but Section 3.1 then says 'if p=0.7... a mere 30% of the connections are active'. This confirms p is the sparsity ratio. But Table 1 shows 'p=0.5' for multiple columns labeled k=10, 50, 100, while the baseline is 'p=0.0'. A careful reader can infer that p is the fraction of weights removed, but the transition between calling something a '0.5-sparse network' and the 'connectivity factor' is phrased clunky, especially when Figure 1(a) equates 'fully connected' with '0.0-sparse', making the 'p' notation flip between 'percentage removed' and 'percentage remaining' in different sentences of Section 3.1.

comments.title: Questionable Claim regarding Performance of Sparse vs Full Networks
comments.quote: kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network.
comments.explanation: The authors claim in Section 5.2 and Figure 3 that sparse networks outperform fully connected networks. While this can happen due to regularization (reducing overfitting), the authors present this as an inherent advantage of sparsity without controlling for parameter count or training duration. Since a single-layer NN (784-10) is extremely under-parameterized for MNIST, the observation that a sparse version 'learns faster' is non-trivial and contradicts standard optimization theory (where over-parameterization usually aids convergence speed). The lack of explanation for *why* a sub-network would out-train the full network at initialization—other than a brief mention of 'information limiting' in Section 7.2—makes the claim technically questionable without more rigorous ablation.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper proposes two initialization-time pruning methods—Kstarts and dissipating gradients—with a combination approach that shows promising results on simple architectures and datasets. The motivation is sound, and the general idea of pruning before or during early training to avoid retraining costs aligns with active research directions. The authors correctly identify connections to evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based sensitivity, and their experimental evaluation on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST provides preliminary support for their claims. However, several technical flaws, ambiguous definitions, and inconsistencies in algorithm description and evaluation undermine confidence in reproducibility and strength of conclusions. Key issues include incorrect algorithm implementation logic, unclear mathematical definitions, and overstatement of performance claims based on limited data.

comments.title: Incorrect population definition in Kstarts
comments.quote: _population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_
comments.explanation: The equation suggests element-wise multiplication between the full weight matrix W and K sparse matrices, resulting in K matrices. However, the notation 'W ∗ K − SparseMatrices' is ambiguous and possibly malformed. If interpreted literally, it suggests W multiplied by (K minus some sparse matrices), which makes no sense. The intended meaning appears to be that the population consists of W multiplied element-wise by each of the K sparse masks. This is a standard way to enforce sparsity, but the notation is mathematically incorrect. The use of '∗' may denote element-wise multiplication, but the expression 'K − SparseMatrices' implies subtraction, which contradicts the context. Even considering OCR error, the dash is consistently used as a hyphen in other parts, making 'K-SparseMatrices' a likely intended compound term. However, standard notation would require clearer formatting, e.g., \{ W \odot M_k \}_{k=1}^K. The lack of clarity impedes precise understanding of the population construction.

comments.title: Fitness function undefined and inconsistent with algorithm
comments.quote:
The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2. 

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'equation 2' and a summation-based fitness function, but no Equation 2 is provided in the paper. The condition 'i ∗ c is the size of each individual' is cryptic and undefined—neither i nor c are introduced. Later, in Section 6.1, the authors mention that magnitude-based fitness uses the sum of weights, but this is inconsistent with Algorithm 1, which selects the individual with the 'maximum sum (of weights)' without defining what is summed. The algorithm assumes this computation happens, but no formal definition exists. Since fitness drives selection, this missing definition is a critical gap. While one might infer the sum of absolute weights, the lack of precision—and the absence of any formal equation—renders the method underspecified.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 incorrectly updates weights before selection
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the weight update occurs *before* selecting which sparse matrix (individual) to use for the next generation. This creates a logical flaw: if weights are already updated using gradients, then overwriting W with an individual (a sparse version of W) erases the update. The intended mechanism—pruning via mask selection—should apply the mask *before* or *during* the forward/backward pass, not after updating weights. Applying the mask after update disrupts training dynamics. The correct order should be: select mask → apply to weights → run network → compute gradients → update masked weights. The current order suggests a fundamental misunderstanding of when sparsity should be enforced, undermining the method's validity.

comments.title: Dissipating gradients algorithm resets W using unclamped accumulated gradients
comments.quote: W ← W*accumulated dW;
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, after applying a threshold (setting accumulated dW to 0 if < 1e-4, else 1), the code uses accumulated dW as a mask. However, the variable 'accumulated dW' is initialized as a gradient accumulator (continuous values), then thresholded to 0 or a small value, not to 1. The condition 'else accumulated dW ← 1' is absent—the text says it becomes 1, but the pseudocode says 'else accumulated dW ← 1;' is not written. Looking at the OCR-like text: 'else accumulated d W ← 1;', it *is* present. So the mask should be binary. But then 'W ← W * accumulated dW' applies the binary mask to the weights. However, this is post-update: weights are updated normally in the loop, then zeroed based on gradient activity. This means gradients were computed and used to update weights that are later zeroed, wasting computation. More importantly, once zeroed, these weights remain zero and can never be reactivated, which may be intended. But the method does not clarify whether gradients are masked during backprop—this is crucial. If not, gradients are computed for zeroed weights in next epoch, which is inefficient and inconsistent with true pruning. The method is underspecified regarding when and how the mask is enforced during training.

comments.title: Mismatch between p-sparsity definition and examples in Figure 1
comments.quote: If p=0.7, figure 1d, a mere 30% of the connections are active between two network layers. In short p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix
comments.explanation: The text states that p is the fraction of connections removed (i.e., sparsity level), so p=0.7 means 70% of connections are zero, 30% active. However, in Section 3.1, it says: 'if p = 0.0 then the network is fully connected'—correct—and 'if p=1.0, then no connections'—also correct. Then it says: 'If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain'—this implies 50% *active*, so sparsity is 50%, meaning p=0.5 is correct. The issue arises in the caption: 'A sample 0.5-Sparse network layer'—this suggests the network is 0.5-sparse, i.e., p=0.5. But the text then says: 'p determines percentage of 0’s', which is consistent. However, in Table 1, p=0.5 corresponds to 50% zeros. So the definition is consistent internally. The confusion arises only in the phrasing: 'p ≈ 0.5' used in Section 7.2 could be mistaken as 50% sparsity, but that’s correct. No actual inconsistency—context resolves it.

comments.title: Table 1 contains likely OCR errors in numerical values
comments.explanation: The use of '_.' instead of '.' strongly suggests OCR corruption. In context, '28_._41' should be '28.41', etc. This affects all entries. One value, '14_._83_±_6_._169', has an asymmetric decimal (6.169 vs others with one decimal). More concerningly, the accuracy jumps from 28% at p=0.0, k=50 to 50.5% with same sparsity but higher k—this is plausible. However, at p=0.5, k=100, accuracy drops to 14.83% at 100 iterations, which seems unusually low compared to k=50 (46.19%). While possible due to instability at high K, the drop is sharp. No explanation is given. However, since the trend reverses at 1k iterations (82.13%), it may reflect slower convergence. Given OCR signs and no other data, we accept these as intended values, though presentation is flawed.

comments.title: Claim of outperforming fully connected networks lacks support
comments.quote: kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network.
comments.explanation: This claim in Section 5.2 is strong: a sparse method outperforms the dense baseline. However, Table 1 shows that at p=0.0 (fully connected), accuracy at 10k iterations is 89.21% for k=100, while for p=0.5, it's 89.08%—slightly lower. At earlier iterations, some p=0.5 settings exceed p=0.0, e.g., at 1k: 82.11% vs 60.71%. So short-horizon gains exist, but final performance does not exceed the fully connected case. The authors may refer to faster learning, but the claim says 'performs better', which implies overall accuracy. This overstates the evidence. The faster convergence is valid, but ultimate performance does not surpass the baseline. This is a questionable claim that could mislead readers.

comments.title: Combination method not algorithmically described
comments.quote: Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the combination outperforms individual methods, but no algorithm or pseudocode is provided for the combination. It says 'fix p for Kstarts' and then apply dissipating gradients, but it's unclear whether the methods are applied sequentially or in parallel. Are the masks combined via AND or OR? Is Kstarts run first to select a mask, then dissipating gradients applied on that? Or are both pruning criteria applied independently? The lack of procedural detail makes reproduction impossible. Given that the combination is claimed to be the best method, this omission is significant.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper proposes two methods for pruning neural networks at initialization (K-starts and dissipating gradients) along with their combination. While the idea of pruning at initialization is valuable, the paper suffers from significant technical flaws. The algorithms contain bugs, the mathematical foundations are questionable (particularly the fitness function based on weight sum), and the experimental evaluation is limited to simple architectures and datasets. The writing quality is poor throughout, making it difficult to follow the technical details. The claims about performance seem overstated given the limited scope of experiments.

comments.title: Critical bug in dissipating gradients algorithm
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 has a critical bug in the handling of accumulated gradients. When accumulated dW < 0.0001, it sets accumulated dW to 0 globally, not for individual weights. This would zero out ALL weights in the next step when applying W ← W*accumulated dW. The algorithm should maintain separate accumulated gradients for each weight. Additionally, setting accumulated dW to 1 for other cases is arbitrary - why not keep the actual accumulated value? This makes the method unreliable and potentially destructive.

comments.title: Fundamentally flawed fitness function
comments.quote: The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: Using the sum of weights as a fitness function is fundamentally flawed. Neural network weights can be positive or negative, and their sum doesn't indicate the 'fitness' of a sparse pattern. The authors themselves acknowledge this issue in Section 6.1, stating 'This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless'. A proper fitness function should be based on validation loss or gradient information, not weight magnitude.

comments.title: Missing key equations
comments.quote: such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: Equation 2 is referenced multiple times but never shown in the paper. Similarly, equations 3 and 4 for the dissipating gradients method are referenced in Section 2.2 but not provided. Without these equations, readers cannot fully understand or reproduce the methods. The authors should ensure all referenced equations are included.

comments.title: Inconsistent and unclear mathematical notation
comments.quote: _population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_
comments.explanation: The mathematical notation is inconsistent and unclear. The minus sign appears to be a typo (should probably be 'K SparseMatrices'). The use of underscores and inconsistent formatting makes it difficult to parse. Standard mathematical notation would be clearer, e.g., Population = {W ⊙ M_i | i=1..K} where M_i are the sparse matrices and ⊙ denotes element-wise multiplication.

comments.title: Overstated performance claims
comments.quote: kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network.
comments.explanation: The claim that sparse networks outperform fully connected networks is overstated given the limited experimental setup. The experiments only use simple architectures (single-layer and three-layer networks) on MNIST/Fashion MNIST. Table 1 shows only marginal improvements in some cases (e.g., 89.21% vs 88.40% for p=0.5, k=100 at 10k iterations), and the fully connected network might catch up with more training. The claim should be qualified to reflect the limited scope.

comments.title: Underspecified elimination frequency
comments.quote: Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.
comments.explanation: The elimination frequency is underspecified. Why 5 generations? Is this hyperparameter tuned? The algorithm uses 'every 5 iterations' but the text mentions 'generations'. The relationship between iterations and generations is unclear. This parameter choice significantly affects the algorithm's behavior but lacks justification or analysis.

comments.title: Confusing method description in dissipating gradients
comments.quote: in each epoch we sum gradients over and eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated.
comments.explanation: The description is confusing - 'eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated' is redundant and unclear. It should specify that weights with accumulated gradients below a threshold are pruned. The method description needs to be clearer about exactly which weights are eliminated and when.

comments.title: Table 1 formatting and clarity issues
comments.explanation: Table 1 has formatting issues that make it hard to interpret. There appear to be duplicate columns for p=0.5, and the header structure is unclear. The table would be clearer with proper headers and consistent formatting. Additionally, the standard deviation values are not clearly marked (e.g., '12_._29_±_3_._79' uses unconventional notation).

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper attempts to address the important problem of pruning at initialization, but it suffers from significant technical and expository shortcomings. The presentation of the algorithms, particularly in sections 2 and 4, lacks the level of formal definition and mathematical rigor expected for a machine learning paper, leading to significant ambiguity regarding the actual implementation of the proposed methods. Several key variables and equations are left undefined (e.g., indices for fitness functions and missing equation labels), and the descriptions of algorithmic logic (like the fitness function evaluation in K-starts) contain logical gaps. The empirical results, while interesting, are limited to very small datasets and networks, and the high-level conclusions are somewhat speculative given the underspecified nature of the experiments.

comments.title: Missing formal definition of the Fitness Function equations
comments.quote: where i * c is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'equation 2' and 'equation 1' for defining the fitness function and population size, but the provided text contains placeholders '??' for these equations. Furthermore, the prose description 'sum of each individual' is mathematically underspecified; it is unclear if this refers to the L1-norm of the weights, the L2-norm, or something else. This makes the core evolutionary mechanism impossible to reproduce.

comments.title: Inconsistency in weight update/pruning logic
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, the pruning logic involves zeroing out elements of the weight matrix W based on whether their accumulated gradients are below a threshold. However, this is described as being performed at every epoch. If a weight is zeroed out in an early epoch, its gradient in subsequent epochs will likely remain zero (or near-zero), meaning once a weight is pruned, it is permanently locked out of training. The paper does not clarify if this is intended as a permanent pruning mechanism or if weights can be 'resurrected'. The notation W*accumulated dW implies element-wise multiplication, but applying this repeatedly without maintenance of a 'mask' means the weight matrix itself is being modified, which is a significant architectural change that needs better justification.

comments.title: Ambiguous connectivity factor p definition
comments.quote: In short p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix shown in equation ??.
comments.explanation: The authors define p as the percentage of connections to be removed. If p=0.5, they say 50% of connections remain. However, they previously stated 'if p=0.7... a mere 30% of the connections are active'. This is consistent, but their later usage, such as '1-p percent active connections' in the Algorithm 1 block, creates a potential sign flip ambiguity. It is unclear if p is a 'sparsity ratio' (target zeros) or 'connection ratio' (target non-zeros). Given the inconsistent referencing, a reader might be confused about which value to pass to the initialization function.

comments.title: Unjustified claim regarding evolutionary algorithm adaptation
comments.quote: The approach is adopted from how evolutionary algorithms find the best individual.
comments.explanation: The authors claim inspiration from evolutionary algorithms, but the mechanism of 'simply picking the one with the highest sum' and 'dropping the one with the lowest fitness every 5 iterations' is a deterministic greedy selection, not a standard evolutionary search (which typically requires crossover, mutation, and selection). The paper overstates the sophistication of the heuristic by equating it with existing evolutionary biology-inspired optimization techniques without justifying why this specific greedy pruning qualifies as an evolutionary adaptation.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting combination of evolutionary search and gradient dissipation for pruning at initialization. The core intuition is sound and the experimental scope covers standard baselines. However, the manuscript contains critical technical gaps and logical inconsistencies that severely impact reproducibility and validity. Key mathematical formulations (Equations 2, 3, 4) are entirely missing, the pseudocode for dissipating gradients contains type mismatches and implements a global rather than element-wise mask, and a primary experimental claim directly contradicts the provided data. Furthermore, the authors' own design analysis reveals that their chosen fitness function effectively breaks the evolutionary selection process, yet they do not explain how this was mitigated. Substantial revision of the methodology sections and reconciliation of results with claims are required.

comments.title: Missing core mathematical formulations (Equations 2, 3, 4)
comments.quote: the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2. In equation 3 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch. In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.
comments.explanation: I initially suspected these equations were lost during PDF-to-text extraction. However, checking the surrounding prose confirms they carry specific mathematical roles: Eq. 2 defines the fitness ranking criterion for Kstarts, while Eqs. 3 and 4 formalize gradient accumulation and thresholding for the dissipating gradients method. The authors correctly acknowledge the concepts, but without the actual formulas, the selection rule and pruning threshold remain mathematically undefined. A reader cannot reconstruct the update mechanisms or reproduce the experiments without them. This is a major gap that breaks methodological continuity.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 pseudocode contains type mismatches and global masking
comments.explanation: I was immediately concerned by the type mismatch and logic in this block. `dW` represents a gradient tensor/matrix, yet it is compared directly to a scalar threshold `0.0001`. Mathematically, this requires an aggregation function (e.g., `‖dW‖ < ε` or `mean(dW)`). Furthermore, reassigning the tensor to a scalar (`0` or `1`) and multiplying `W` by it applies a global binary mask to the entire weight matrix, rather than pruning individual weights as described in the text. The intended element-wise mask operation (e.g., `W ⊙ (|dW| > ε)`) is missing. The algorithm is mathematically invalid and unreproducible as stated.

comments.title: Table 1 results contradict Section 7.1 claim about K
comments.quote: From the experiments done so far lower K ( _≈_ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( _≈_ 100).
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly claim that a smaller population size (K=10) outperforms or matches a larger one (K=100). I checked Table 1 to verify this statement. At 1k iterations, K=10 yields 82.11 ± 2.97 accuracy, while K=100 yields 82.13 ± 3.63. At 10k iterations, K=10 gives 89.07 ± 0.40, whereas K=100 gives 89.21 ± 0.38. The empirical data actually shows that higher K performs slightly better or equal, with notably lower variance. The textual claim directly contradicts the provided results, suggesting a likely authorial typo or misinterpretation that needs correction.

comments.title: Section 6.1 admits fatal flaw in Kstarts fitness function
comments.quote: Magnitude: As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated.
comments.explanation: Reading Section 6.1, I was confused by the authors' self-critical assessment of their chosen fitness function. They explicitly state that their magnitude-based approach 'renders elimination to be pointless' and causes the initial sparse matrix to perpetually propagate, effectively freezing the evolutionary search. This admission directly undermines the core mechanism of Kstarts, which relies on dynamic selection and elimination across generations. Despite this stated flaw, the paper reports successful results without explaining whether a different fitness metric was actually used in the final experiments, or how this critical stagnation issue was circumvented. The methodology and design discussion are logically inconsistent.

comments.title: Ambiguous population generation notation in Equation 1
comments.quote: _population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_
comments.explanation: I initially wondered if `∗` denoted convolution, but the context implies a set of transformed weights. The notation `W ∗ K − SparseMatrices` ambiguously suggests a single output rather than a population of K distinct matrices. Standard evolutionary notation for this setup would use an indexed operation (e.g., `P_g = {W ⊙ M_k | k = 1, ..., K}`) or explicitly state element-wise multiplication across a batch. As written, it misleads readers regarding the population generation step and complicates attempts to implement the method correctly, though this is a moderate clarity issue rather than a mathematical error.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes two pre-training pruning methods (K-starts and Dissipating Gradients) with a combination approach. While the core ideas are interesting, the manuscript suffers from multiple critical issues that severely undermine reproducibility and clarity. Key equations are missing, algorithms are ambiguously described, and a technical error exists in the dissipating gradients algorithm. The claims about performance are only weakly supported, and the combination method is underspecified. The paper requires substantial revision and additional detail before it can be considered for publication.

comments.title: Missing equations for fitness function and gradient accumulation
comments.quote: The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: Equation 2 is never shown in the text; only a placeholder exists. Similarly, equations 3 and 4 in Section 2.2 are referenced but not present. These equations are central to the two proposed methods. Without them, the reader cannot understand or replicate the fitness evaluation and the gradient accumulation procedure. This is a major omission that undermines the entire contribution.

comments.title: Ambiguity in K-starts algorithm: interaction between weight updates and mask selection
comments.quote: In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration.
comments.explanation: The description suggests that the population is recomputed each iteration by applying each sparse mask to the current weights W. However, Algorithm 1 indicates that W is updated via gradient descent and then set to 'the individual with maximum sum'. It is unclear whether this 'individual' is a binary mask or a masked weight matrix, and whether subsequent iterations use the original W (before masking) or the masked version. The pseudocode does not specify how the masks in KI are used to generate the population after the first iteration. This ambiguity makes it impossible to reproduce the method.

comments.title: Technical error in Dissipating Gradients algorithm: scalar conditional on accumulated gradient
comments.quote: if accumulated d W < 0 . 0001 then accumulated d W ← 0 ; else accumulated d W ← 1; end W ← W*accumulated dW;
comments.explanation: The variable 'accumulated dW' is presumably a tensor of the same shape as W, accumulating per-weight gradients. However, the conditional compares the entire tensor to a scalar threshold, which is not meaningful. The intended operation is almost certainly an element-wise comparison. This error suggests a fundamental misunderstanding of how to implement such pruning, and the provided pseudocode cannot be executed as written.

comments.title: Underspecified combination method
comments.quote: Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well.
comments.explanation: It is not clear how the two methods are combined: is it a union of eliminated weights, or an intersection? The phrase 'further eliminate' suggests union, but the order and specific integration are not described. Additionally, how are conflicting decisions resolved (e.g., a weight kept by K-starts but marked for elimination by dissipating gradients)? The lack of detail prevents replication.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for population generation
comments.quote: population = W ∗ K − SparseMatrices
comments.explanation: This equation is unclear. It likely intends an element-wise (Hadamard) product between W and each of K sparse masks, but the notation 'K − SparseMatrices' is syntactically confusing. Moreover, no operator (e.g., ⊙) is defined, and the hyphen could be misinterpreted as a minus sign. The paper should formally define the operation to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Fitness function ambiguity: sum of weights without sign consideration
comments.quote: W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights)
comments.explanation: In Section 6.1, the paper lists 'Magnitude' as a possible fitness criterion, but Algorithm 1 simply says 'maximum sum (of weights)'. If weights can be negative, summing them directly could cancel out. It is ambiguous whether the absolute value is taken or some other magnitude measure. This affects the selection process and should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: Questionable claim about lower K outperforming higher K
comments.quote: From the experiments done so far lower K ( ≈ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( ≈ 100).
comments.explanation: Table 1 shows accuracies for k=10, 50, 100 at 10k iterations as 89.07, 89.08, 89.21 respectively, which are essentially identical within standard deviation. The claim that lower K outperforms is not supported; at best they are comparable. This generalization is overstated based on the presented data.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper proposes two novel initialization-based pruning methods, K-starts and dissipating gradients, offering a potentially simpler alternative to lottery ticket training. The experimental results on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST demonstrate promise, particularly for computational efficiency. However, the submission suffers from significant readability issues caused by OCR artifacts and transcription errors (e.g., garbled LaTeX, missing equations, inverted logic in algorithms). More critically, the discussion section presents a quantitative contradiction between the text claiming that lower 'K' values perform as well as higher ones and Table 1, which shows significantly better performance for K=50 than K=10. Furthermore, the theoretical justification for why evolutionary selection during initialization yields performant sparse networks is underdeveloped. The paper requires a revision to correct these technical errors and clarify the methodology.

comments.title: Quantitative Contradiction in Discussion Section
comments.quote: lower K ( _≈_ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( _≈_ 100).
comments.explanation: I initially looked for the experimental evidence supporting this claim. In Section 7.1, the authors state that lower K values (around 10) are superior or at least comparable to higher K values (around 100). However, looking closely at Table 1 (under row 'iter 1k' for the MNIST dataset), the accuracy for K=10 is 63.39 ± 6.94, while the accuracy for K=50 is 82.11 ± 2.97. K=50 performs significantly better than K=10. This contradicts the text's qualitative conclusion that lower K outperforms higher K. The reader is left wondering which data interpretation is correct, undermining the authors' analysis of the hyperparameter sensitivity.

comments.title: Corrupted Mathematical Notation and Missing Equations
comments.quote:
_population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_ 

(1) 

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.
comments.explanation: The LaTeX rendering in the extracted text is heavily corrupted. Equation 1 is missing the superscript on K (likely intended as $K_{sparse}$) and the hyphen appears to be a formatting artifact. More importantly, the description for Equation 2 is completely unreadable ('i * c'), preventing me from verifying the proposed fitness function. The paper relies on these equations to define the core methodology, and their illegibility forces the reader to guess the intended meaning rather than assessing the math rigorously.

comments.title: Underspecified Pruning Logic in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: if  accumulated d W <  0 . 0001  then accumulated d W ← 0 ; else accumulated d W  ← 1; end W  ← W*accumulated dW;
comments.explanation: While reading Algorithm 2 for the 'Dissipating Gradients' method, the update rule `W  ← W*accumulated dW` raises a significant concern. If `accumulated dW` represents a boolean mask (0 or 1) derived from gradient thresholds, this line correctly masks weights (sets them to 0). However, the accumulation step `accumulated d W ← accumulated d W+ dW` adds gradients to a running sum. If this sum ever exceeds the threshold (0.0001), it is set to 1 and never decreased. This implies that once a gradient is 'detected', the mask stays '1' for the rest of the run. While this might be the intended behavior for pruning 'dead' weights, the mathematical operation `W * accumulated_dW` is applied in a way that overrides the standard weight update rule of Adam, which is confusing. The logic relies on a specific interpretation of how gradients accumulate over an epoch that is not standard.

comments.title: Insufficient Justification for Evolutionary Selection
comments.quote: The approach is adopted from how evolutionary algorithms find the best individual... Depending on the Neural Network architecture, fitness criteria can be based on magnitude of network weights, magnitude of gradient accumulation over an epoch or a combination of both.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce K-starts as an evolutionary approach to finding the 'lottery ticket' (high-performance sparse network). However, they do not justify *why* selecting the matrix with the highest sum of weights (magnitude) during random initialization leads to a network that can learn the task. If the weights are initialized randomly, selecting the one with the largest sum is effectively random (since sums of random matrices follow a distribution). The intuition that this mimics 'natural selection' is not supported by the mathematical properties of the initialization. Readers need to understand the link between the fitness function in Eq 2 and the training dynamics of the network.

comments.title: Questionable Claim about Sparsity vs. Full Connectivity
comments.quote: The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters... kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that for low iteration counts, the sparse network (K-starts) outperforms the fully connected network (FC). While this is plausible due to regularization (less overfitting) and better conditioning of the optimization problem in high dimensions, the statement is made as a mere observation without deeper analysis. The reader might infer this is a generic property of sparsity, which is not true for all datasets or architectures. A more rigorous explanation of *why* the sparsity helps convergence speed on MNIST (e.g., local minima distribution) would strengthen this claim.
